nativeRange.habitat: Sheltered rocky reefs, intertidal reef flats, lagoon and seaward reefs; tends to occur around coral or over barren areas

diet.notes: Has been documented eating algae, hard-shelled invertebrates, small crustaceans, molluscs, polychaetes, tunicates, and even soft and stony corals in the wild. In captivity, generally advised to feed a varied meaty diet (squid, clams, krill, mysis, hard-shelled shrimp) interspersed with algae to wear down continuously growing beak-like teeth.

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by 探索墾丁 (ekt.idv.tw), 3072x2048. Tack-sharp full lateral view against dark water; vivid reddish-brown body with dense pale-blue/turquoise and white spots, netted markings, orange eye-ring and blue radiating eye-lines all clearly shown. Strongest hero image.
Photo by 探索墾丁 (ekt.idv.tw), 3072x2048. Sharp three-quarter frontal angle showing the sharpnose profile, blue eye-lines and spotting; good complementary angle to the lateral hero shot.
Photo by Nick Hobgood, 2250x1800. Crisp in-situ reef shot showing prominent turquoise longitudinal lines plus blue spotting; adds variety with natural reef context.

summary: The Blue Spotted Puffer is a small Indo-Pacific sharpnose puffer, reaching around 10 to 12 cm, with dark netted markings and vivid pale blue spots. Generally considered hardy and personable, it is not reef-safe and carries tetrodotoxin in its skin and organs.
careNotes: The Blue Spotted Puffer is one of the more manageable Canthigaster tobies, topping out around 10-12 cm and adaptable to mid-sized reef-grade systems (250 L+ generally advised given its constant cruising and territorial streak). It is best kept singly — conspecifics tend to fight, and mixing with other Canthigaster species is also commonly discouraged. Although marketed as 'reef-safe with caution,' field and aquarist accounts agree it has been known to nip soft corals, LPS polyps, feather dusters, ornamental shrimp, snails, and hermit crabs; treat it as not reef-safe for any prized invertebrate display. Like all tetraodontids, the species sequesters tetrodotoxin (TTX) in skin and organs; it can also release toxins into the water when severely stressed or dying, so handle gently — transfer in a container rather than a net to avoid air-puffing — and never house with anything you cannot afford to lose if a die-off event occurs. Diet should be varied and include hard-shelled foods (clams, krill, shrimp) to wear down its continuously growing fused beak.
breedingNotes: The Blue Spotted Puffer is rarely bred in captivity. In the wild, Canthigaster solandri is oviparous and facultatively monogamous, with adults often found in bonded pairs. No established home-aquarium spawning protocol has been widely documented.

fish.breedingNotes: Canthigaster solandri is pair-forming and oviparous in the wild. No established captive-breeding protocol has been documented.
